researchFindings.claim: The debris hut is the most important no-fire shelter to master; it uses body heat alone to maintain core temperature in cold conditions by trapping air in layers of leaves and grasses
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core purpose statement — sets priority and explains the insulation mechanism

researchFindings.claim: Choose a site with abundant nearby debris (leaf litter, dead leaves, grasses, moss); avoid areas under widow makers, near ant or wasp nests, and away from creeks or low points where water pools
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Site selection — material availability and hazard avoidance are essential first steps

researchFindings.claim: Prop one end of a long ridgepole (longer than your body) on a stump, rock, or forked branch about waist height, with the other end on the ground, creating a sloped main spine
Lean shorter branches ('ribs') closely against both sides of the ridgepole to create a ribbed frame, then weave a latticework of smaller branches and vines to hold the debris layer
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Frame and lattice construction — lattice is what holds the debris pile from sliding off

researchFindings.claim: Pile a minimum of 3 feet (1 meter) of debris on the outside of the frame; in temperatures near 0°F, 3–4 feet is needed for adequate insulation
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Minimum insulation depth — the most commonly miscalculated step; people build too thin and freeze

researchFindings.claim: Best insulating materials are dead leaves and dry grasses; their shape traps air pockets; conifer needles (pine, spruce) are poor insulators and should not be used as the primary debris layer
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Material selection — conifer needle warning is counterintuitive and important

researchFindings.claim: Create a floor/bed of 1–2 feet of compressed debris inside the shelter; ground conduction is a major heat loss pathway and the interior floor insulation is as important as the outer layer
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Ground insulation — skimping on interior bedding is the most common failure mode

researchFindings.claim: Size the interior just large enough to fit your body; an oversized shelter cannot retain body heat and will stay cold
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Sizing guidance — directly affects thermal performance

researchFindings.claim: Create a small entrance just large enough to crawl through at the high end; create a door plug by stuffing a shirt, bag, or armful of leaves to block the opening after entering
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Entrance and door plug — sealing the entry is critical to heat retention

researchFindings.claim: Enter feet first to avoid disturbing the debris walls; seal the entrance behind you with the door plug immediately
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Entry technique — prevents dislodging debris and maintains insulation integrity

researchFindings.claim: A well-built debris hut can maintain core body temperature in conditions down to 0°F without fire; it is more thermally effective than a lean-to in extreme cold
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Best Survival Shelter: A-Frame, Debris, Teepee, Lean-To?
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Performance claim — key differentiator from lean-to; helps users understand when to choose this shelter

researchFindings.claim: Building a functional debris hut in the western USA can be difficult due to lack of leaf litter; this shelter type works best in forested areas with abundant organic ground debris
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Regional limitation — important for users in arid or alpine environments

identifiedGaps.impact: Sources vary; a well-built debris hut takes 2–4 hours, but this is environment-dependent and not cited from a single authoritative source
identifiedGaps.suggestion: Omit time estimate or say 'plan for several hours' without a hard number

recommendation: Write a concise action_card for building a debris hut as a no-fire emergency overnight shelter. Emphasize the minimum insulation depth (3 feet exterior, 1–2 feet interior floor), material selection (leaves over conifer needles), body-sized interior, and entrance plug. Include warning that this shelter type requires abundant leaf litter and does not work well in arid/mountain terrain without debris. Steps should be 7–9 items.
